title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot investigates the death of Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, a wealthy widow of a shipbuilder who resided in Stone Quarry House, a Victorian mansion featuring an underground garden built from an abandoned quarry. Although her death was attributed to heart failure following exertion in her garden, Poirot explores the possibility of foul play. The investigation reveals a controversy regarding a codicil to her will; while her previous wills left her property to her nephew and his wife, the Drakes, this new codicil left everything to a young nurse from Central Europe. The Manchester law firm Fuller, Harrison, and Leedbetter identified the codicil as a forgery, noting that the nurse had experience mimicking handwriting from her time writing letters for Lady Lintin-Smith. Following intense questioning by lawyers, the nurse fled the country, leaving her belongings behind. There is a suggestion that the nurse may have served the victim a drink with a strange or bitter taste prior to her death.

Poirot also inquires about Charlotte Belfield, a sixteen-year-old shop assistant found dead with multiple head injuries near a mining area. Two young men, Peter Gordon and Thomas Heddle, were identified as potential suspects. Peter Gordon is an unemployed man with a history of petty theft and associations with delinquents, while Thomas Heddle is a shy, anxious stationery store worker with a stammer. Although jealousy was considered a possible motive, both men had alibis: Heddle's mother claimed he was home with her, and Gordon's friends provided an alibi, though the credibility of these statements was questioned.
